1.pericope_label: The Song of Moses and the Lamb
1.exposition: Chapter 15 is the shortest in Revelation, and the smallest of the great heavenly interludes that stand before each cycle of judgment — yet it is the hinge on which the bowls turn. John sees another sign in heaven, great and marvellous: seven angels having the seven last plagues, called "last" because in them the wrath of God is brought to its completion. But before a single bowl is poured, the vision pauses — not on the torment of the wicked, but on the triumph of the redeemed. Beside a sea of glass mingled with fire stand those who conquered the beast, its image, and the number of its name, holding the harps of God, and they sing the song of Moses the servant of God and the song of the Lamb. Heaven interprets the plagues before earth ever feels them, and its interpretation is doxology: "Great and marvellous are thy works … just and true are thy ways." The most terrible judgment-series in Scripture opens in worship, and closes this opening scene with the promise that all nations shall come and worship the God whose righteous acts are made manifest.
1.original_language: The "sea of glass" of the throne-room (Revelation 4:6) returns here memigmenē pyri ("mingled with fire") — the calm crystal now shot through with the fire of judgment, or, as the earliest voices heard it, the fire of trial and martyrdom through which the conquerors passed. Their praise is an ōdē (a sung "ode"), and it is one hymn under two names: "the song of Moses and the song of the Lamb" — not two anthems but a single song in which the old deliverance and the new are joined. The plagues are eschatas ("last"), and with them the wrath is already etelesthē ("is finished"), a proleptic aorist — the end spoken of as accomplished because in God's purpose it is certain.
1.historical_context: The "song of Moses" is the hymn Israel sang on the far shore of the Red Sea, after they had passed through on dry ground and watched Pharaoh's army drown (Exodus 15); a second song of Moses, the covenant witness of Deuteronomy 32, vindicates God's justice against the nations. For John's churches, pressed by the imperial cult under Domitian, the promise is plain: as Pharaoh's tyranny could not finally hold the people of God, neither can Rome's. latin-fathers Victorinus of Pettau and Bede, the earliest commentators preserved in this catena, read the fiery sea as the font of baptism "consecrated by the fire of the Holy Ghost," and the conquerors as the Church standing fast on its confession — the persecuted reading their own perseverance into the vision. The couplets of the song are themselves a mosaic of older praise (Psalms 111:2; Psalms 86:9; Jeremiah 10:7), so that the new song is sung in the old covenant's own words.
1.christ: The redeemed sing one song of two names because the deliverance Moses only prefigured is accomplished in Christ: the exodus through the sea finds its antitype in the blood of the Lamb, "by which they overcame" (Revelation 12:11). He is praised as "King of saints" — or, in the better-attested reading, "King of the nations" — and the promise that all nations shall come and worship reaches back to the pledge that in Abraham's seed every nation would be blessed (Genesis 12:3) and forward to the shared throne of God and the Lamb in the new creation (Revelation 22:3). The terror of the bowls is thus bracketed by the Lamb who was slain, so that even wrath is read in the light of the cross.

verses.note: Another sign in heaven, great and marvellous: seven angels having the seven last plagues. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson mark the fresh vision, looking back to the signs of Revelation 12; the seven bowls run parallel to the seals and trumpets but now press to the finish, for etelesthē ("is finished") declares the wrath complete. latin-fathers Victorinus of Pettau and Bede hear behind the number the sevenfold covenant curse — the wrath that "ever smites the rebellious people with seven plagues" (Leviticus 26:21), striking "perfectly," completely. grammatical-historical Ellicott presses the word plague back to Egypt: as the ten plagues exposed Pharaoh's gods, these fall on the beast-power — no longer warnings to the wavering but strokes on the hardened. wesleyan-arminian Wesley adds that God, having long borne his enemies with much longsuffering, now sends his wrath forth to the uttermost.

A sea of glass mingled with fire, and on it the conquerors of the beast, holding the harps of God. grammatical-historical Robertson notes that the peaceful crystal sea of Revelation 4:6 is now mingled with fire, adding both the splendour and the menace of judgment to the scene. puritan Matthew Henry hears the Red Sea beneath the image: the faithful stand as Israel stood while the congealed waters, lit by the pillar of fire, seemed to burn — waters that would soon dissolve upon Pharaoh. grammatical-historical Ellicott draws the contrast the whole book presses: the beast rose out of a turbulent sea (Revelation 13:1), but before the throne lies the calm, fire-flushed sea of God's righteous counsels; the life that was "a discord to the world" rises into true music on the harps of God (Revelation 14:2).

They sing the song of Moses the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb. grammatical-historical Barnes explains that, as Moses taught Israel to celebrate their rescue from Egypt (Exodus 15), so the redeemed celebrate a deliverance whose circumstances strongly resemble it; grammatical-historical Robertson shows the song woven wholly from the Old Testament — "great" and "marvellous" from the Psalms, "Almighty" from Amos, "just and true" from the song of Deuteronomy 32. On the disputed title the witnesses divide with the manuscripts — "King of saints," "of nations," or "of ages" — but all agree the point stands: God's works are magnified and his ways owned as just. grammatical-historical Adam Clarke sums it: every step God takes in grace or providence is according to justice, and he keeps every threat and every promise, bound to it by his truth.

Who shall not fear thee, O Lord, and glorify thy name? grammatical-historical Robertson reads the rhetorical question with the ring of great poetry — none can withhold the reverence that is God's due. The ground is his uniqueness: grammatical-historical Barnes notes "thou only art holy," so pure that, by comparison, no one else is holy at all. Then the horizon widens: all nations shall come and worship before thee, "for thy judgments are made manifest." grammatical-historical Ellicott catches the tense — the angels of judgment are only now going forth, yet the nations' worship is spoken of as already accomplished, a divine certainty; the revealing of God's righteous acts is itself what draws the peoples home. wesleyan-arminian Wesley hears in the line a glorious testimony to the future conversion of the heathen: now a little flock worships, but all the nations shall come.

witnesses.point: As Moses taught Israel to sing after the Red Sea, the redeemed celebrate a deliverance strongly resembling it; "thou only art holy" means God is so pure that, by comparison, none else is holy.
The victors' song is woven entirely from Old Testament praise, and its rhetorical "Who shall not fear thee?" has the ring of great poetry.
The saints stand on the sea of glass as Israel stood by the Red Sea, its congealed waters lit to fire by the pillar and soon to dissolve upon Pharaoh; new mercies call forth new hymns of praise.
The wrath strikes the obstinate with seven plagues, that is "perfectly," as Leviticus says; the conquerors stand steadfast in the faith upon their baptism, exulting before God.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.point: The song's declaration that "all nations shall come and worship" is no formality but the book's genuine hope: the new exodus accomplished by the Lamb draws the nations, through the costly witness of the martyrs, into the worship of the one true God.

1.application: The vision disciplines our speech about judgment. The conquerors do not gloat over the plagues; they sing, confessing that God's ways are "just and true." To "conquer" the beast was never to escape death but to refuse its mark and keep faith — and the reward is to stand, harp in hand, on the far shore. When we are tempted to speculate about wrath, heaven teaches us instead to worship.

5.exposition: The worship ends and the sanctuary opens: after this I looked, and the temple of the tabernacle of the testimony in heaven was opened. Out of it come the seven angels with the seven plagues, robed like priests in pure and white linen with golden girdles, and one of the four living creatures hands them seven golden vials full of the wrath of God, who liveth for ever and ever. Then the temple is filled with smoke from the glory of God and from his power, and no man is able to enter until the seven plagues are fulfilled. The whole scene is an authorization. The bowls do not rise from the chaos of history; they issue from God's own dwelling, handed over by the worshipping creatures, so that what earth will suffer as catastrophe is shown to originate in the holiness of God. And the sanctuary that had been opened as a refuge is now, for the duration of the judgment, closed.
5.original_language: "The temple of the tabernacle of the testimony" renders ho naos tēs skēnēs tou martyriou — skēnē tou martyriou being the Septuagint's standing name for the wilderness "tent of witness" that housed the tablets of the testimony. The vessels are phialai — not the deep, narrow "vials" of the old English, but shallow temple bowls, the same word used of the golden censers of incense in Revelation 5:8; now they brim not with the prayers of the saints but with wrath. The temple is filled with kapnos ("smoke"), the ancient token of the divine presence, and none can enter achri telesthōsin ("until they are finished") — the very verb of completion that sealed the wrath in v.1.
5.historical_context: "The tent of witness" points every synagogue-trained hearer to the wilderness tabernacle that held God's covenant law (Exodus 38:21; Numbers 1:50) — the very law that Rome and the beast-worshippers have flouted, so that the plagues come from the sanctuary of the covenant they despised. grammatical-historical Adam Clarke and Ellicott note that the angels' linen and golden sashes are priestly vestments (compare the glorified Christ, Revelation 1:13, and the priestly garments of Exodus 28): these are ministers of the sanctuary performing a liturgical act, not a rampage. The smoke that fills the temple deliberately echoes the glory-cloud that filled the tabernacle at its dedication (Exodus 40:34–35) and Solomon's temple (1 Kings 8:10–11), and the smoke of Isaiah 6:4, where the prophet's vision of glory issued in a message of hardening judgment.
5.christ: The sanctuary filled with the glory of God recalls the glory that "tabernacled" among us in the incarnate Word (John 1:14) — the same verb, skēnoō, as the "tent" of witness here. And the closed sanctuary stands in pointed contrast to the access Christ opens: through his flesh believers have "a new and living way" into the holy places (Hebrews 10:19–20). The bowls fall on those who refused that way. The Lamb who shares the throne (Revelation 5:6) is the one from whose presence the wrath now proceeds, so that the crucified Mediator is also the righteous Judge — the very Christ who is both the sanctuary and the door.

verses.note: The temple of the tabernacle of the testimony in heaven was opened. grammatical-historical Barnes takes the naos as the holy of holies itself, the peculiar dwelling of God, now thrown open so that the command to judge issues directly from his presence; grammatical-historical Robertson and Clarke recall the "tent of witness" of Numbers 1:50, the tabernacle that held the tables of the law. puritan Matthew Henry reads the opened sanctuary as the sign that in these judgments God is fulfilling the prophecies and promises of his covenant, and answering the prayers of his people offered through their great High Priest.

The seven angels came out of the temple … clothed in pure and white linen, and having their breasts girded with golden girdles. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note the priestly array — the white linen the dress of holiness and purity, the golden girdle the mark of dignity and priestly office, like the glorified Christ of Revelation 1:13: the agents of judgment are themselves holy, and their commission sacred. grammatical-historical Adam Clarke marks that they come "out of the temple" precisely to show they are sent from God himself, habited as priests after the pattern of Exodus 28.

One of the four beasts gave unto the seven angels seven golden vials full of the wrath of God, who liveth for ever and ever. grammatical-historical Barnes corrects the old rendering: the word is phialē, a broad, shallow bowl like those of the temple service (Revelation 5:8), not a narrow "vial" — a cup of wrath handed over to be poured out. wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the vessels are broader at the top than the bottom, and that their being "full of the wrath of God who liveth for ever" adds greatly to the dreadfulness. grammatical-historical Ellicott hears significance in the giver: the bowls come from one of the living creatures who represent creation, for it is through creation that God's righteous law visits the rebellious (Psalms 79:6). Barnes adds that the appeal to God's eternity marks the certainty of the wrath — he is not, like men, forced to abandon a purpose left long unaccomplished.

The temple was filled with smoke from the glory of God, and from his power; and no man was able to enter … till the seven plagues … were fulfilled. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson identify the smoke as the token of the divine presence, as at Sinai (Exodus 19:18) and in Isaiah 6:4; Robertson calls it, aptly, a symbolic "smoke screen" barring the sanctuary for the time. grammatical-historical Ellicott draws the sober point from Isaiah's vision: the days of warning are over, and none may enter to make intercession — the plagues must now run their course on those who trifled with conviction. wesleyan-arminian Wesley adds that the smoke of glory is a sign both of favour and of judgment, and that none — not even those who ordinarily stood before God — could go into the inmost place until the wrath was fulfilled.

witnesses.point: The "temple" is the holy of holies itself; the golden "vials" are really broad temple bowls of wrath; the smoke is the token of the divine presence, as at Sinai, barring all approach until the purpose is accomplished.

The angels are priestly ministers of the sanctuary; drawing on Isaiah 6, the closed, smoke-filled temple means the days of warning are past and judgment must fall on those who spurned the call to repent.
In judging the antichristian power God fulfils his covenant and answers his people's prayers; the angels do everything in a pure and holy manner, armed with the wrath of God against his enemies.
The cloud of glory that once signaled God's favour in tabernacle and temple here signals judgment; both proceed from his power, and in both he is glorified.

external.name: G.K. Beale
external.point: The plagues proceed from the heavenly "tabernacle of witness," and the smoke that bars entry echoes the Sinai and tabernacle theophanies; the sevenfold wrath deliberately recalls the covenant curses of Leviticus 26, marking the bowls as the covenant-Lord's measured and just recompense rather than arbitrary fury.

5.application: That "no man was able to enter the temple until the seven plagues were fulfilled" is a sober word: there is a point at which the door of intercession is shut and judgment becomes irrevocable. The same chapter that began in worship ends with a warning that God's patience, though long, is not endless. The pastoral edge is urgency — that the wrath proceeds from "God who liveth for ever" means no oppressor outlasts him, and that the saints' prayers ascend while the door still stands open. Pray and witness now.

_meta.title: Just and True Are Your Ways
reflection: The most terrible judgment in the book opens not with the suffering of the wicked but with the worship of the redeemed, who sing that God's ways are "just and true" (15:3). What does it do to your own view of God's judgments to hear them framed first by a song?

To "conquer" the beast was not to escape death but to refuse its mark and keep faith (v.2). Where are you being pressed to take the mark — to give ultimate loyalty to something other than God — and what would faithful endurance look like there?

The song foresees that "all nations shall come and worship" once God's righteous acts are made manifest (v.4). How does the hope that judgment finally serves the worship of the nations reshape the way you pray for a world still under God's patience?
